past_medical_history: Episode of left arm and hand numbness and tingling lasting 3 weeks, approximately 8 months ago, which resolved spontaneously without medical evaluation
Mild iron deficiency anemia, managed with oral supplementation
family_history: Maternal aunt: diagnosed with multiple sclerosis at age 38
No family history of epilepsy or malignancy

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 2-3 glasses of wine per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a suburban home
social_history.exercise: Runs 3-4 times per week, recently noticed decreased stamina

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent without dysarthria or paraphasic errors. Attention intact; serial sevens performed accurately. Short-term memory intact, recalling 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No evidence of cognitive impairment on bedside assessment.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual acuity reduced in the right eye to 20/80 with Snellen chart (left eye 20/20). Right relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) present. Color desaturation on the right (red appears washed out). Fundoscopic exam reveals mild right optic disc pallor. CN III-VI: Extraocular movements full bilaterally, mild pain with right eye adduction noted. No nystagmus. CN V: Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all divisions bilaterally. CN VII: Face symmetric, no weakness. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX-XII: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag intact, tongue midline, sternocleidomastoid and trapezius full strength.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone mildly increased in bilateral lower extremities with subtle spasticity at the knees. Strength: upper extremities 5/5 bilaterally in all muscle groups. Lower extremities: hip flexion 4+/5 bilaterally, knee extension 4+/5 bilaterally, ankle dorsiflexion 5/5 bilaterally. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased vibration sense at bilateral great toes (diminished compared to medial malleoli). Light touch intact throughout. Pinprick intact. Proprioception intact in fingers and toes. Lhermitte sign present: flexion of the neck produces electric shock-like sensation radiating down the spine and into the legs.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk). Achilles 3+ bilaterally. Plantar responses extensor (upgoing) bilaterally (positive Babinski sign). Sustained ankle clonus (4-5 beats) bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly impaired on the right with subtle ataxia. Rapid alternating movements normal in upper extremities.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait mildly spastic with circumduction noted bilaterally, more prominent on the right. Tandem gait impaired with tendency to veer to the right. Romberg sign positive.
neurological_exam.additional: Uhthoff phenomenon reported by patient: symptoms worsen after hot showers or exercise. Lhermitte sign reproducible on examination. Right relative afferent pupillary defect clearly demonstrable with swinging flashlight test.

patient.chief_complaint: Painful vision loss in the right eye with worsening leg weakness over the past 10 days
patient.history_present_illness: This 31-year-old right-handed woman presents to the neurology service after being admitted through the emergency department with a 10-day history of progressive, painful vision loss in her right eye accompanied by worsening bilateral leg weakness. The visual symptoms began as a dull, aching pain behind her right eye that worsened with eye movements, followed two days later by progressive blurring of vision in that eye. Over the ensuing week, her vision continued to deteriorate to the point where she describes seeing only shapes and shadows with the right eye. She also noticed that colors appeared washed out and dull when viewed through the affected eye compared to the left. Concurrently, she developed increasing heaviness and stiffness in both legs, making it difficult to climb stairs or run, activities she previously performed without difficulty. She reports two episodes of urinary urgency over the past week, once resulting in incontinence, which she found deeply distressing and out of character. She also describes an unusual electric shock-like sensation radiating down her spine and into her legs whenever she bends her neck forward, which began approximately five days ago. Upon further questioning, she recalls an episode approximately eight months ago in which her left arm and hand became numb and tingled for about three weeks before gradually resolving on its own. She did not seek medical attention at that time, attributing it to a pinched nerve from sleeping in an awkward position. She also notes that her symptoms seem to worsen after hot showers or during exercise, with her vision becoming more blurry and her legs feeling heavier in the heat. She denies any recent illness, fever, headache, seizures, difficulty swallowing, or bowel dysfunction. She has no history of rashes, joint pains, oral ulcers, or sicca symptoms. She has not traveled recently and denies any tick exposure.

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, bilateral, predominantly along the lateral ventricles
findings.size: Multiple lesions ranging from 5 mm to 18 mm

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense (several with T1 black holes indicating chronic axonal loss)
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, ovoid orientation perpendicular to ventricles (Dawson fingers morphology)
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Two lesions demonstrate ring-like gadolinium enhancement indicating active inflammation

findings.location: Juxtacortical white matter, left frontal and right parietal regions
Right middle cerebellar peduncle and left brachium pontis (infratentorial)

signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Left brachium pontis lesion shows faint gadolinium enhancement

findings.location: Right optic nerve, retrobulbar segment extending 15 mm

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal within the right optic nerve
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Intense gadolinium enhancement of the right optic nerve

findings.mass_effect: Mild swelling of the right optic nerve
findings.borders: Ill-defined margins consistent with active inflammation

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level, dorsal columns
findings.size: Spanning one vertebral segment, less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (lesion spans less than 3 vertebral segments)
Ventricles and sulci normal for age
No mass lesion, midline shift, or hydrocephalus
mri.impression: Multiple T2/FLAIR hyperintense white matter lesions in characteristic distribution for demyelinating disease: periventricular (with Dawson fingers morphology), juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord involvement. Three enhancing lesions indicating active inflammation, including right optic nerve enhancement consistent with acute optic neuritis. Non-enhancing lesions suggest prior subclinical disease activity. Findings satisfy the McDonald 2017 criteria for dissemination in space (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord involvement) and dissemination in time (simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions). Overall appearance is highly characteristic of multiple sclerosis.
recommended_actions: Correlate with CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
Consider visual evoked potentials to confirm optic nerve demyelination
Check NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies to exclude mimics
Recommend follow-up MRI in 3-6 months to monitor disease activity

labs.interpretation: All laboratory panels within normal limits. CBC unremarkable with no evidence of active infection or hematologic abnormality. BMP normal. Inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) within normal limits, which is typical for MS as it is not associated with systemic inflammation. ANA negative, arguing against systemic lupus erythematosus. Vitamin B12 and TSH normal, ruling out metabolic causes of myelopathy. NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies both negative, significantly reducing the likelihood of neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder and MOG antibody disease.

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: 5 unique oligoclonal bands present in CSF, absent in serum (positive)
special_tests.myelin_basic_protein: 8.4 ng/mL (normal: <4 ng/mL; elevated, consistent with active demyelination)

csf.interpretation: CSF demonstrates mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (12 cells/uL), mildly elevated protein, and normal glucose with preserved CSF-to-serum glucose ratio. Five unique oligoclonal bands detected in CSF but absent in serum, indicating intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis. IgG index significantly elevated at 0.89, further confirming intrathecal antibody production. Elevated myelin basic protein consistent with active demyelination. Overall CSF profile is highly characteristic of multiple sclerosis with evidence of active inflammatory demyelination.

Visual_Evoked_Potentials.clinical_significance: Prolonged P100 latency indicating demyelination of the right optic nerve, consistent with optic neuritis
Reduced amplitude indicating significant optic nerve dysfunction on the right
Significant interocular latency difference supporting unilateral optic nerve demyelination

output.interpretation: Visual evoked potentials demonstrate markedly prolonged P100 latency and reduced amplitude in the right eye, with normal responses from the left eye. The significant interocular latency difference of 20 ms confirms right optic nerve demyelination consistent with acute-on-chronic optic neuritis. These findings provide electrophysiological evidence supporting the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.
abnormal_values_summary: Right eye P100 latency prolonged at 128 ms (normal <115 ms)
Right eye P100 amplitude reduced at 3.2 uV (normal >5 uV)

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4, dorsal columns
findings.size: Spanning one vertebral segment, occupying less than half the cross-sectional area

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: No enhancement, suggesting chronic or subacute lesion

findings.location: Thoracic spinal cord at T6-T7, lateral column predominantly left-sided
findings.size: Spanning less than one vertebral segment

signal_characteristics.T2_axial: Lateral column involvement, left greater than right
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Faint gadolinium enhancement suggesting subacute activity

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (no lesion spans 3 or more vertebral segments)
No evidence of cord compression or extrinsic mass
Short-segment lesions in typical MS distribution
output.impression: Two short-segment T2 hyperintense spinal cord lesions identified at C3-C4 and T6-T7, both occupying less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord in a pattern characteristic of multiple sclerosis. The thoracic lesion shows faint enhancement suggesting subacute inflammatory activity. No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis to suggest NMOSD. Findings further support the diagnosis of MS with evidence of dissemination in space.
recommended_actions: Correlate with brain MRI findings and clinical presentation
Consider somatosensory evoked potentials if clinically indicated
Follow-up imaging in 3-6 months to monitor lesion activity

OCT_RNFL_Thickness.clinical_significance: Significant thinning of retinal nerve fiber layer in the right eye consistent with optic nerve axonal loss from optic neuritis
Normal RNFL thickness in the left eye
Temporal quadrant thinning most prominently affected, typical of MS-related optic neuritis

OCT_RNFL_Thickness.test: Right eye ganglion cell layer thickness
OCT_RNFL_Thickness.clinical_significance: Ganglion cell layer thinning indicating retrograde neuronal degeneration

output.interpretation: Optical coherence tomography demonstrates significant thinning of the retinal nerve fiber layer in the right eye (average 68 um, normal 80-110 um) with predominant temporal quadrant involvement. Ganglion cell layer analysis also shows thinning in the right eye. Left eye measurements are within normal limits. These findings are consistent with axonal loss secondary to right optic neuritis and support the clinical diagnosis of demyelinating optic neuritis in the context of multiple sclerosis.
abnormal_values_summary: Right eye average RNFL thickness reduced at 68 um (normal 80-110 um)
Right eye temporal quadrant RNFL reduced at 48 um (normal 60-85 um)
Right eye ganglion cell layer thinning at 62 um (normal 75-100 um)

NMO_MOG_Antibody_Panel.clinical_significance: Negative AQP4 antibody effectively rules out NMOSD in the appropriate clinical context
Negative MOG antibody rules out MOGAD

output.interpretation: Both aquaporin-4 (NMO-IgG) and MOG antibodies are negative by cell-based assay, which is the most sensitive and specific methodology. These results effectively exclude neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) and MOG antibody-associated disease (MOGAD) as alternative diagnoses. In combination with the clinical presentation, MRI findings, and positive CSF oligoclonal bands, the overall picture strongly favors a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.

findings.size: Stable in size compared to prior study

signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Previously enhancing periventricular lesions no longer enhance, indicating resolved active inflammation
Faint gadolinium enhancement indicating recent inflammatory activity

additional_observations: One new lesion in the right corona radiata not present on prior study, confirming ongoing disease activity
Previously enhancing lesions have resolved, indicating treatment response or natural evolution
Right optic nerve enhancement has resolved
output.impression: Follow-up MRI demonstrates one new T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesion in the right corona radiata with faint enhancement, confirming ongoing subclinical disease activity despite resolution of the previously enhancing lesions. This new lesion activity underscores the need for initiation of disease-modifying therapy to prevent further relapses and disability accumulation.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with any new symptoms
Continue routine MRI surveillance every 6-12 months

output.query: multiple sclerosis relapsing-remitting disease-modifying therapy guidelines 2024

results.title: Practice guideline recommendations summary: Disease-modifying therapies for adults with multiple sclerosis
results.summary: Recommends initiating DMT in patients with relapsing forms of MS. Higher-efficacy therapies should be considered in patients with active disease (clinical relapses and/or MRI activity).

results.title: McDonald Criteria 2017 Revision
results.source: International Panel on Diagnosis of Multiple Sclerosis
results.summary: Defines diagnostic criteria requiring demonstration of dissemination in space and time. CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for dissemination in time in patients with a single clinical event and MRI evidence of DIS.

results.title: High-efficacy vs escalation approach in RRMS
results.summary: Growing evidence supports early initiation of high-efficacy DMTs (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab, alemtuzumab) over traditional escalation approaches, particularly in patients with poor prognostic factors such as high lesion burden, spinal cord lesions, or incomplete recovery from relapses.

output.summary: Current guidelines recommend prompt initiation of disease-modifying therapy in patients diagnosed with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis. The choice between moderate-efficacy (interferon-beta, glatiramer acetate, dimethyl fumarate, teriflunomide) and high-efficacy therapies (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab, cladribine) should be individualized based on disease activity, prognostic factors, and patient preferences. Patients with high lesion burden, spinal cord involvement, or aggressive presentation may benefit from early high-efficacy therapy.

contraindications: Active hepatitis B infection (must screen prior to initiation)
warnings: Combined oral contraceptive: No significant interaction, but counsel regarding family planning as ocrelizumab is teratogenic
Live vaccines are contraindicated during treatment; ensure vaccinations are up to date prior to initiation
Screen for hepatitis B and C, tuberculosis, and varicella immunity before starting
Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) risk: very low but requires monitoring
output.formulary_status: Formulary approved with prior authorization for confirmed RRMS diagnosis
alternatives: Natalizumab 300 mg IV every 4 weeks (higher efficacy but JCV-associated PML risk)
Ofatumumab 20 mg SC monthly (similar mechanism, self-administered)
Dimethyl fumarate 240 mg PO BID (moderate efficacy, oral)
Cladribine 3.5 mg/kg total over 2 years (immune reconstitution therapy)

differential.key_features: AQP4 antibodies negative, short-segment spinal cord lesions (not longitudinally extensive), Dawson fingers morphology on brain MRI, oligoclonal bands positive — all favor MS over NMOSD
MOG antibodies negative, periventricular lesions with Dawson fingers are atypical for MOGAD, CSF oligoclonal bands more characteristic of MS
Normal ESR and CRP, no systemic inflammatory signs, lesion distribution and morphology classic for MS not vasculitis, negative ANA
No leptomeningeal enhancement, no pulmonary findings, no elevated ACE, CSF profile more consistent with MS than sarcoidosis
Lesion distribution periventricular with Dawson fingers and infratentorial involvement is not seen in migraine; gadolinium enhancement, CSF oligoclonal bands, and clinical syndrome all point to MS

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including prior neurological episodes (dissemination in time), current symptoms timeline, and review of systems for red flags
optimal_actions.expected_finding: History reveals prior episode of left arm numbness lasting 3 weeks (8 months ago), consistent with a prior demyelinating event and dissemination in time
Right RAPD, reduced visual acuity OD, spastic paraparesis, Lhermitte sign, Babinski signs bilaterally, ankle clonus — multifocal findings suggesting CNS demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain and cervical/thoracic spine with gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord lesions with Dawson fingers morphology, enhancing and non-enhancing lesions satisfying McDonald 2017 criteria for DIS and DIT

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, 5 CSF-unique oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG index (0.89), elevated myelin basic protein

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs including CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, TSH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All within normal limits, helping exclude systemic inflammatory or metabolic causes

optimal_actions.action: Order NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4 antibodies) and MOG antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both negative, excluding NMOSD and MOGAD

optimal_actions.action: Initiate acute treatment with IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3-5 days for the acute optic neuritis and relapse
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in visual acuity and reduction in optic nerve inflammation; speeds recovery from acute relapse

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to confirm optic nerve demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged right P100 latency (128 ms) with reduced amplitude confirming right optic nerve demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Discuss disease-modifying therapy options and initiate DMT (high-efficacy therapy recommended given high lesion burden and spinal cord involvement)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient counseled on options; decision made to initiate high-efficacy DMT such as ocrelizumab, natalizumab, or ofatumumab

optimal_actions.action: Refer to neuro-ophthalmology for ongoing visual monitoring and OCT
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Baseline OCT shows RNFL thinning in the right eye, establishing baseline for monitoring

optimal_actions.action: Plan follow-up MRI at 3-6 months to establish new baseline on DMT and monitor for disease activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Follow-up MRI will serve as new baseline for monitoring treatment efficacy

optimal_actions.action: Refer to MS specialist/comprehensive MS center, and arrange multidisciplinary support including physical therapy, occupational therapy, and psychological support
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Comprehensive care plan established for long-term management

critical_actions: Identify the prior episode of left arm numbness as a likely prior demyelinating event (dissemination in time)
Order MRI brain and spine with gadolinium to demonstrate dissemination in space and time
Perform lumbar puncture and identify CSF oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index
Exclude NMOSD and MOGAD with antibody testing (NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies)
Apply McDonald 2017 criteria to confirm the diagnosis of MS
Initiate IV corticosteroids for acute relapse treatment
Initiate disease-modifying therapy to prevent further relapses and disability
contraindicated_actions: Discharging without initiating acute relapse treatment (IV corticosteroids)
Diagnosing clinically isolated syndrome without recognizing the prior episode as a second clinical event
Starting disease-modifying therapy without excluding NMOSD (some DMTs such as interferon-beta can worsen NMOSD)
Administering live vaccines if planning to start immunosuppressive DMT
Performing an MRI without gadolinium contrast (would miss enhancing lesions critical for DIT assessment)
Attributing symptoms to functional neurological disorder or anxiety without completing the diagnostic workup
key_reasoning_points: The combination of acute optic neuritis (painful vision loss with RAPD) and prior episode of limb numbness establishes two clinical events separated in time, meeting dissemination in time clinically

MRI findings with periventricular (Dawson fingers), juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord lesions satisfy McDonald 2017 criteria for dissemination in space across at least 2 of 4 characteristic locations
Simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on MRI independently satisfies dissemination in time by imaging
CSF oligoclonal bands (5 bands unique to CSF) provide additional supportive evidence and can substitute for DIT in some clinical scenarios under McDonald 2017
Negative NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies are essential to exclude important MS mimics before initiating MS-specific therapy
Lhermitte sign indicates cervical cord demyelination, correlating with the C3-C4 spinal cord lesion on MRI
Uhthoff phenomenon (heat sensitivity) is highly characteristic of demyelinating disease
High lesion burden with spinal cord involvement and incomplete recovery warrants consideration of high-efficacy DMT rather than moderate-efficacy escalation approach
Normal inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) and negative ANA help exclude systemic autoimmune and inflammatory conditions that can mimic MS

metadata.author: NeuroBench Generator
clinical_pearls: Uhthoff phenomenon and Lhermitte sign are clinical hallmarks of MS
Short-segment spinal cord lesions favor MS over NMOSD
Dawson fingers (periventricular ovoid lesions perpendicular to ventricles) are pathognomonic for MS on MRI
CSF oligoclonal bands are found in >90% of MS patients
